properties.name: Great Smoky Mountains
properties.note: The highest, wettest, and most biodiverse range in the eastern U.S. — ancient, rounded summits cloaked in cloud forest.

properties.name: Black Mountains
properties.note: A short, steep Blue Ridge cross-range holding Mount Mitchell, the highest summit in eastern North America.

properties.name: Blue Ridge
properties.note: The long eastern wall of the Appalachians, running from Georgia to Pennsylvania — the Blue Ridge Parkway traces its crest.

properties.name: Shenandoah
properties.note: The Virginia Blue Ridge above the Shenandoah Valley, protected as Shenandoah National Park.

properties.name: Allegheny Mountains
properties.note: The high eastern edge of the Appalachian Plateau, holding Spruce Knob and the headwaters of the Potomac.

properties.name: Cumberland Plateau
properties.note: The deeply dissected southern Appalachian Plateau of Tennessee and Kentucky — flat-topped, gorge-cut tableland.

properties.name: Catskills
properties.note: A dissected plateau of flat-lying Devonian rock carved into peaks — not folded mountains at all.

properties.name: Poconos
properties.note: A glaciated northeastern-Pennsylvania escarpment of the Appalachian Plateau.

properties.name: Adirondacks
properties.note: A billion-year-old dome of Grenville basement — geologically a southern outlier of the Canadian Shield, not the Appalachians proper.

properties.name: Green Mountains
properties.note: Vermont's spine — metamorphosed rock stacked during the Taconic and Acadian orogenies.

properties.name: White Mountains
properties.note: New Hampshire's high country — Acadian granite holding the tallest summits in the Northeast.

properties.name: Berkshires & Taconics
properties.note: Western Massachusetts' marble valleys and schist hills — a southern continuation of the Green Mountains.

properties.name: Longfellow Mountains
properties.note: Maine's high country, culminating at Katahdin — the northern end of the Appalachian Trail.

properties.name: Chic-Choc Mountains
properties.note: The high spine of Quebec's Gaspé Peninsula — arctic-alpine tundra on the roof of the Canadian Appalachians.

properties.name: Notre Dame Mountains
properties.note: The Quebec Appalachians paralleling the south shore of the St. Lawrence — a direct continuation of the Green Mountains across the border.

properties.name: Mount Carleton Highlands
properties.note: New Brunswick's forested interior highlands, crowned by Mount Carleton, the highest point in the Maritimes.

properties.name: Cape Breton Highlands
properties.note: A windswept plateau at the northern tip of Nova Scotia, dropping in sea cliffs along the Cabot Trail.

properties.name: Long Range Mountains
properties.note: The northern terminus of the Appalachians — ancient basement uplifted along Newfoundland's west coast, with the fjords and mantle rock of Gros Morne.
